name: CI/CD & IaC
description: Pipelines and infrastructure as code: integration, delivery strategies, Terraform and GitOps. 30-question seed set, growing to 300.

questions.text: CI (continuous integration) means…
options: Deploying to production several times a day in practice
Merging and automatically testing code changes often
Writing code without any version control
Manually integrating changes once a month
questions.explanation: Small changes, merged frequently, verified by an automated build and tests each time.

questions.text: A CI pipeline typically starts when…
options: Code is pushed or a pull request is opened
The production server runs out of memory
A scheduled maintenance window opens in practice
A developer manually clicks deploy
questions.explanation: A push or PR triggers the pipeline: build, test, and report status back automatically.

questions.text: The main benefit of automated tests in a pipeline is…
options: They make the code compile faster
They replace the need for code review
They catch regressions before code merges
They reduce the size of the repository in practice
questions.explanation: Fast feedback: a break is caught in minutes, not in production a week later.

questions.text: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) means…
options: Coding directly on production servers
Defining infrastructure in version-controlled files
Writing application logic in config files
Storing code inside the infrastructure
questions.explanation: Servers, networks and policies described in files you review, version and re-apply — not clicked by hand.

questions.text: Terraform is primarily a tool for…
options: Monitoring application logs
Running unit tests in parallel
Building container images in practice as a rule
Provisioning infrastructure declaratively
questions.explanation: You declare the desired infrastructure; Terraform plans and applies the changes to reach it.

questions.text: CD can stand for continuous delivery, meaning…
options: Every change auto-deploys to production
Code is continuously deleted after testing
Developers deliver code once per quarter
Changes are always ready to deploy at the click of a button
questions.explanation: Delivery keeps main always releasable; deployment (the other CD) pushes automatically all the way to prod.

questions.text: A build artifact is…
options: A bug introduced during the build
The output produced by a successful build
A server that runs the pipeline in practice
A comment left in the pipeline config
questions.explanation: The compiled binary, image or package the pipeline produces and passes to later stages.

questions.text: Version control (e.g. Git) in a CI/CD flow provides…
options: Encryption of network traffic in practice as a rule
Automatic scaling of the application
The single source of truth for code and history
A place to store production databases
questions.explanation: Everything flows from the repo: pipelines trigger on it, and history makes every change auditable.

questions.text: A pipeline 'stage' is…
options: A branch in the Git repository
A logical grouping of steps that run in order
A type of automated test
A physical server in the build farm
questions.explanation: Build, test, deploy — stages structure the pipeline and often gate one another.

questions.text: Rolling back a deployment means…
options: Restarting the CI server
Pausing all traffic to the service in practice
Reverting to a previously working version
Deleting the application entirely
questions.explanation: When a release misbehaves, return to the last known-good version while you investigate.

questions.text: Terraform state is used to…
options: Map declared resources to real-world ones
Log every command that was run
Cache downloaded provider plugins in practice
Store your cloud provider credentials
questions.explanation: State links config to actual infrastructure so Terraform knows what to change, keep or destroy.

questions.text: A key reason to run 'terraform plan' before 'apply' is to…
options: Speed up the apply step afterwards
Encrypt the state file
Preview changes before they happen
Format the configuration files
questions.explanation: Plan shows what will be created, changed or destroyed — your last chance to catch a destructive diff.

questions.text: Idempotency in IaC means…
options: Applying the same config repeatedly yields the same result
Every apply creates new resources in practice as a rule in practice
Each run uses a unique identifier
Configuration cannot be changed once applied
questions.explanation: Re-applying an unchanged config changes nothing — the property that makes IaC safe to run often.

questions.text: A pipeline secret (e.g. an API token) should be…
options: Committed to the repo in an encrypted file
Stored in a secret store and injected at runtime
Emailed to whoever runs the pipeline
Hardcoded in the pipeline YAML for simplicity
questions.explanation: Secret managers or masked CI variables — never in the repo, never in logs.

questions.text: Blue-green deployment works by…
options: Running two colours of server for aesthetics in practice
Splitting users randomly forever
Deploying only on alternating days
Switching traffic between two identical environments
questions.explanation: Deploy to the idle environment, test, then flip traffic — instant cutover and instant rollback.

questions.text: A canary release means…
options: Rolling back automatically every hour
Deploying to all users at once in practice as a rule
Testing only in a staging environment
Exposing a new version to a small subset first
questions.explanation: A small slice gets the new version; if metrics hold, you widen it — limiting blast radius.

questions.text: Terraform modules exist to…
options: Run tests against infrastructure
Encrypt provider credentials
Store the state file remotely
Package and reuse configuration
questions.explanation: Reusable, parameterised building blocks — write a VPC once, instantiate it many times.

questions.text: A remote state backend is preferred over local state because it…
options: Removes the need for a plan step in practice
Runs applies faster than local state
Enables team sharing and state locking
Encrypts the Terraform binary
questions.explanation: Shared state plus locking stops two engineers from applying at once and corrupting it.

questions.text: A pipeline 'gate' or manual approval is used to…
options: Speed up the deployment process
Automatically merge pull requests in practice as a rule
Require human sign-off before a sensitive stage
Cache build dependencies
questions.explanation: A deliberate pause — often before production — where a person takes responsibility for proceeding.

questions.text: Artifact versioning matters because it…
options: Makes artifacts smaller on disk
Lets you deploy and roll back to an exact build
Speeds up the build stage in practice as a rule in practice
Encrypts the artifact in transit
questions.explanation: Immutable, versioned artifacts mean the thing you tested is exactly the thing you ship — and can revert to.

questions.text: Terraform state locking prevents…
options: Concurrent applies from corrupting the state
Providers from being upgraded
Secrets from being stored in state
The state file from growing too large in practice
questions.explanation: A lock (e.g. via DynamoDB or the backend) serialises applies so two runs can't clash on the same state.

questions.text: A GitOps workflow makes the source of truth…
options: A Git repository that a controller reconciles to the cluster
The CI server's local disk
A ticket in the issue tracker in practice as a rule in practice
A manually maintained spreadsheet
questions.explanation: Commit the desired state; an agent (Argo CD, Flux) continuously converges the cluster to match it.

questions.text: 'terraform import' is used to…
options: Bring existing infrastructure under Terraform management
Download provider plugins
Copy state between backends in practice as a rule in practice
Merge two configurations
questions.explanation: Adopt resources created outside Terraform into state so it can manage them going forward.

questions.text: A count/for_each pitfall in Terraform is that using count with a list means…
options: State locking is disabled in practice as a rule in practice
Resources can never be updated
The plan step is skipped entirely
Removing a middle item reindexes and recreates others
questions.explanation: count keys by position; for_each keys by a stable identifier, so removing one doesn't churn the rest.

questions.text: Ephemeral (disposable) CI runners improve security by…
options: Running builds much faster
Sharing caches across every job
Never needing credentials
Discarding all state after each job
questions.explanation: A fresh environment per job means a compromised build can't linger and poison the next one.

questions.text: A deployment strategy's 'bake time' refers to…
options: How long the image takes to build
A period of monitoring a release before proceeding
How long secrets remain valid
The time to warm up a cache
questions.explanation: Let the canary or new version soak while you watch metrics, so problems surface before full rollout.

questions.text: Pipeline caching (e.g. of dependencies) trades speed against the risk of…
options: Stale or poisoned cache entries
Slower first builds in practice as a rule
Larger artifact sizes
Losing the state file
questions.explanation: A wrong or tampered cache entry silently corrupts builds — scope and key caches carefully, and expire them.

questions.text: Terraform workspaces are commonly used to…
options: Manage multiple state instances of one config
Store secrets separately from state in practice
Replace modules entirely
Encrypt the backend
questions.explanation: One config, several states — e.g. dev and prod — though separate backends are often clearer at scale.

questions.text: A supply-chain attack on a CI pipeline typically…
options: Guesses the repository password
Overloads the runner with traffic
Compromises a dependency or build step to inject malicious code
Deletes the state file
questions.explanation: Poison a dependency or the build itself and the malware ships signed as legitimate — pin and verify everything.

questions.text: Drift detection in IaC means…
options: Measuring how fast the pipeline runs
Finding differences between declared and actual infrastructure
Tracking how often code is merged
Detecting slow-running tests
questions.explanation: Someone clicked a change in the console; drift detection surfaces the gap so you can reconcile or re-apply.
